content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob have an array of AAA of NNN elements, and will play a game on it. They alternate turns, with Alice going first.

On each turn:


Let MMM denote the current length of AAA.

If M=1M = 1M=1, the game stops. Otherwise, the current player must make a move.
On Alice's turn, she must choose integers LLL and RRR (1≤L<R≤M1 \leq L \lt R \leq M1≤L<R≤M), delete the elements AL,AL+1,…,ARA_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_RAL​,AL+1​,…,AR​ from the array,

and insert ∣AL+AL+1+…+AR∣|A_L + A_{L+1} + \ldots + A_R|∣AL​+AL+1​+…+AR​∣ in their place.

Note that ∣x∣|x|∣x∣ denotes the absolute value of xxx.
On Bob's turn, he must choose integers LLL and RRR (1≤L<R≤M1 \leq L \lt R \leq M1≤L<R≤M), delete the elements AL,AL+1,…,ARA_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_RAL​,AL+1​,…,AR​ from the array, and insert (AL+AL+1+…+AR)(A_L + A_{L+1} + \ldots + A_R)(AL​+AL+1​+…+AR​) in their place.
That is, Alice must choose some subarray, delete it, and insert the absolute value of its sum in its place.

Bob will do the same, but insert just the sum (and not its absolute value).

Note that since L<RL \lt RL<R, the length of the array will decrease after each move.

Alice wants to maximize the value of the final element, while Bob wants to minimize it.

If both Alice and Bob make their moves optimally, what will the value of the final element be?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the number of elements in AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, denoting the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the value of the final element if both players move optimally.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 4⋅1054 \cdot 10^54⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The array is already of length 111, so no moves will be made.
Test case 222: Since L<RL \lt RL<R, Alice has no choice but to choose the entire array [5,−7][5, -7][5,−7] and replace it with ∣5+(−7)∣=2|5 + (-7)| = 2∣5+(−7)∣=2, at which point the game ends.
Test case 333: One possible sequence of moves is as follows:

Alice chooses L=5L = 5L=5 and R=6R = 6R=6, replacing the subarray [−2,−1][-2, -1][−2,−1] with its absolute sum 333.
The array is now A=[1,1,−2,5,3]A = [1, 1, -2, 5, 3]A=[1,1,−2,5,3].
Bob chooses L=2L = 2L=2 and R=4R = 4R=4, replacing the subarray [1,−2,5][1, -2, 5][1,−2,5] with its sum 444.
The array is now A=[1,4,3]A = [1, 4, 3]A=[1,4,3].
Alice chooses L=2L = 2L=2 and R=3R = 3R=3, turning the array into A=[1,7]A = [1, 7]A=[1,7].
Bob chooses L=1L = 1L=1 and R=2R = 2R=2, turning the array into A=[8]A = [8]A=[8].
